speaker: NURSE
text: Mistress! What, mistress! Juliet!—Fast, I warrant her, she—

speaker: NURSE
text: Why, lamb, why, lady! Fie, you slugabed!

speaker: NURSE
text: Why, love, I say! Madam! Sweetheart! Why, bride!—

speaker: NURSE
text: What, not a word?—You take your pennyworths now.

speaker: NURSE
text: Sleep for a week, for the next night, I warrant,

speaker: NURSE
text: The County Paris hath set up his rest

speaker: NURSE
text: That you shall rest but little.—God forgive me,

speaker: NURSE
text: Marry, and amen! How sound is she asleep!

speaker: NURSE
text: I needs must wake her.—Madam, madam, madam!

speaker: NURSE
text: Ay, let the County take you in your bed,

speaker: NURSE
text: He’ll fright you up, i’ faith.—Will it not be?

speaker: NURSE
text: What, dressed, and in your clothes, and down again?

speaker: NURSE
text: I must needs wake you. Lady, lady, lady!—

speaker: NURSE
text: Alas, alas! Help, help! My lady’s dead.—

speaker: NURSE
text: O, weraday, that ever I was born!—

speaker: NURSE
text: Some aqua vitae, ho!—My lord! My lady!

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: What noise is here?

speaker: NURSE
text: O lamentable day!

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: What is the matter?

speaker: NURSE
text: Look, look!—O heavy day!

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: O me! O me! My child, my only life,

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: Revive, look up, or I will die with thee.

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: Help, help! Call help.

speaker: CAPULET
text: For shame, bring Juliet forth. Her lord is come.

speaker: NURSE
text: She’s dead, deceased. She’s dead, alack the day!

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: Alack the day, she’s dead, she’s dead, she’s dead.

speaker: CAPULET
text: Ha, let me see her! Out, alas, she’s cold.

speaker: CAPULET
text: Her blood is settled, and her joints are stiff.

speaker: CAPULET
text: Life and these lips have long been separated.

speaker: CAPULET
text: Death lies on her like an untimely frost

speaker: CAPULET
text: Upon the sweetest flower of all the field.

speaker: NURSE
text: O lamentable day!

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: O woeful time!

speaker: CAPULET
text: Death, that hath ta’en her hence to make me wail,

speaker: CAPULET
text: Ties up my tongue and will not let me speak.

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: Come, is the bride ready to go to church?

speaker: CAPULET
text: Ready to go, but never to return.—

speaker: CAPULET
text: O son, the night before thy wedding day

speaker: CAPULET
text: Hath Death lain with thy wife. There she lies,

speaker: CAPULET
text: Flower as she was, deflowerèd by him.

speaker: CAPULET
text: Death is my son-in-law; Death is my heir.

speaker: CAPULET
text: My daughter he hath wedded. I will die

speaker: CAPULET
text: And leave him all. Life, living, all is Death’s.

speaker: PARIS
text: Have I thought long to see this morning’s face,

speaker: PARIS
text: And doth it give me such a sight as this?

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: Accursed, unhappy, wretched, hateful day!

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: Most miserable hour that e’er time saw

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: In lasting labor of his pilgrimage!

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: But one, poor one, one poor and loving child,

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: But one thing to rejoice and solace in,

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: And cruel death hath catched it from my sight!

speaker: NURSE
text: O woe, O woeful, woeful, woeful day!

speaker: NURSE
text: Most lamentable day, most woeful day

speaker: NURSE
text: That ever, ever I did yet behold!

speaker: NURSE
text: O day, O day, O day, O hateful day!

speaker: NURSE
text: Never was seen so black a day as this!

speaker: NURSE
text: O woeful day, O woeful day!

speaker: PARIS
text: Beguiled, divorcèd, wrongèd, spited, slain!

speaker: PARIS
text: Most detestable death, by thee beguiled,

speaker: PARIS
text: By cruel, cruel thee quite overthrown!

speaker: PARIS
text: O love! O life! Not life, but love in death!

speaker: CAPULET
text: Despised, distressèd, hated, martyred, killed!

speaker: CAPULET
text: Uncomfortable time, why cam’st thou now

speaker: CAPULET
text: To murder, murder our solemnity?

speaker: CAPULET
text: O child! O child! My soul and not my child!

speaker: CAPULET
text: Dead art thou! Alack, my child is dead,

speaker: CAPULET
text: And with my child my joys are burièd.

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: Peace, ho, for shame! Confusion’s cure lives not

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: In these confusions. Heaven and yourself

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: Had part in this fair maid. Now heaven hath all,

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: And all the better is it for the maid.

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: Your part in her you could not keep from death,

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: But heaven keeps his part in eternal life.

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: The most you sought was her promotion,

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: For ’twas your heaven she should be advanced;

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: And weep you now, seeing she is advanced

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: Above the clouds, as high as heaven itself?

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: O, in this love you love your child so ill

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: That you run mad, seeing that she is well.

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: She’s not well married that lives married long,

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: But she’s best married that dies married young.

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: Dry up your tears, and stick your rosemary

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: On this fair corse, and, as the custom is,

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: And in her best array, bear her to church,

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: For though fond nature bids us all lament,

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: Yet nature’s tears are reason’s merriment.

speaker: CAPULET
text: All things that we ordainèd festival

speaker: CAPULET
text: Turn from their office to black funeral:

speaker: CAPULET
text: Our instruments to melancholy bells,

speaker: CAPULET
text: Our wedding cheer to a sad burial feast,

speaker: CAPULET
text: Our solemn hymns to sullen dirges change,

speaker: CAPULET
text: Our bridal flowers serve for a buried corse,

speaker: CAPULET
text: And all things change them to the contrary.

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: Sir, go you in, and, madam, go with him,

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: And go, Sir Paris. Everyone prepare

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: To follow this fair corse unto her grave.

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: The heavens do lour upon you for some ill.

speaker:
FRIAR
               
              LAWRENCE
text: Move them no more by crossing their high will.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: Faith, we may put up our pipes and be gone.

speaker: NURSE
text: Honest good fellows, ah, put up, put up,

speaker: NURSE
text: For, well you know, this is a pitiful case.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: Ay, by my troth, the case may be amended.

speaker: PETER
text: Musicians, O musicians, Heart’s ease,

speaker: PETER
text: Heart’s ease. O, an you will have me live, play

speaker: PETER
text: Heart’s ease.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: Why Heart’s ease?

speaker: PETER
text: O musicians, because my heart itself plays “My

speaker: PETER
text: heart is full.” O, play me some merry dump to

speaker: PETER
text: comfort me.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: Not a dump, we. ’Tis no time to play

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: now.

speaker: PETER
text: You will not then?

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: No.

speaker: PETER
text: I will then give it you soundly.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: What will you give us?

speaker: PETER
text: No money, on my faith, but the gleek. I will give

speaker: PETER
text: you the minstrel.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: Then will I give you the

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: serving-creature.

speaker: PETER
text: Then will I lay the serving-creature’s dagger on

speaker: PETER
text: your pate. I will carry no crochets. I’ll re you, I’ll fa

speaker: PETER
text: you. Do you note me?

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: An you re us and fa us, you note us.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              MUSICIAN
text: Pray you, put up your dagger and

speaker:
SECOND
               
              MUSICIAN
text: put out your wit.

speaker: PETER
text: Then have at you with my wit. I will dry-beat

speaker: PETER
text: you with an iron wit, and put up my iron dagger.

speaker: PETER
text: Answer me like men.

speaker: PETER
text: When griping griefs the heart doth wound

speaker: PETER
text: And doleful dumps the mind oppress,

speaker: PETER
text: Then music with her silver sound—

speaker: PETER
text: Why “silver sound”? Why “music with her silver

speaker: PETER
text: sound”? What say you, Simon Catling?

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: Marry, sir, because silver hath a

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: sweet sound.

speaker: PETER
text: Prates.—What say you, Hugh Rebeck?

speaker:
SECOND
               
              MUSICIAN
text: I say “silver sound” because musicians

speaker:
SECOND
               
              MUSICIAN
text: sound for silver.

speaker: PETER
text: Prates too.—What say you, James Soundpost?

speaker:
THIRD
               
              MUSICIAN
text: Faith, I know not what to say.

speaker: PETER
text: O, I cry you mercy. You are the singer. I will say

speaker: PETER
text: for you. It is “music with her silver sound” because

speaker: PETER
text: musicians have no gold for sounding:

speaker: PETER
text: Then music with her silver sound

speaker: PETER
text: With speedy help doth lend redress.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              MUSICIAN
text: What a pestilent knave is this same!

speaker:
SECOND
               
              MUSICIAN
text: Hang him, Jack. Come, we’ll in

speaker:
SECOND
               
              MUSICIAN
text: here, tarry for the mourners, and stay dinner.
